One surprising example of reciprocity occurred in the year 2001. After the September 11th attacks on New York City that same year, donations and offers of help poured in from all over the United States. There was one offer, however, that really stood out. It was a large donation from the small town of Columbia, South Carolina. At that time, Columbia had a population of 116,000 people, while the population of New York City was over 8 million people. But the South Carolina town's citizens worked hard to raise $354,000, with which they purchased a state-of-the-art fire truck for New York City's fire department. Why such a large gift from such a small town? When journalists investigated this extraordinary gift, they found that the citizens of Columbia were motivated by reciprocity. Way back in 1867, a group of New York City firemen had sent in a gesture of peace after the Civil War, a state-of-the-art hose carriage to the city of Columbia to replace one that had been destroyed by the Union Army. One hundred and thirty-five years later, the residents of Columbia saw the perfect opportunity to reciprocate in kind. Importantly, reciprocity does not only bind individuals together, but it can transcend individuals and tie together groups, organizations, and whole societies. In fact, every human society teaches its children to give, receive, and then repay as a way to keep them connected. But there's a vital nuance to how reciprocity works. As a sleuth of influence, the key is to be the first one to give in order to activate this powerful principle.
